name: Oshogatsu (Japanese New Year)
traditions: Hatsumode (first shrine visit); eating osechi-ryori (traditional foods); mochi (rice cakes); kadomatsu decorations; nengajo greeting cards.
historicalBackground: Japanese New Year (Shōgatsu) switched from lunar to January 1 in 1873 during the Meiji Restoration. Traditional customs remain deeply observed.
description: Japan's most important holiday, a serene celebration of renewal with shrine visits, traditional feasts, and the quiet beauty of winter.
